summary: Joe Reis argues that while companies are rushing to adopt AI, most haven't even mastered basic BI, which historically has only a 20% success rate. He emphasizes that AI and BI are fundamentally different disciplines with different data requirements, and that the real challenge isn't the technology itself but the underlying difficulty of organizational change and data management.
key_points: AI/BI initiatives share the same ~20% success rate due to fundamental organizational challenges, not technology limitations
AI and BI require different data approaches - BI's tabular data models aren't necessarily useful for ML use cases
Generative AI excels at specific tasks like summarization, translation, and synthesis of unstructured content
The hype cycle will burst, allowing serious practitioners to build useful AI without the noise
Education about AI's actual capabilities vs. limitations is critical for realistic implementation
money_quotes: Most companies want to do AI. Most are barely doing BI.
AI means you get more work done with fewer humans. Instead of hiring workers to complete a task, I can incorporate ML/AI into a workflow and augment or eliminate the pesky humans.

If your BI efforts are flailing, I'm guessing adoption of your dashboards and reports are pretty light or nonexistent. Will throwing a chatbot on top of this same data suddenly make people interested in data and analytics?
Change is hard. Data is hard.
The current hype cycle will burst, and the people building really useful AI and ML can operate in an environment without as many hucksters and bullshit artists.
key_insight: The fundamental challenges of data initiatives—misunderstanding problems, lacking proper data, chasing technology over solutions—remain constant regardless of whether you call it BI, Big Data, or AI, and companies would benefit from realistic expectations rather than hype-driven adoption.
